text: Welcome to this special 3D video.
Here I am trying out your 3D.

You aren't going to get very far with this video if you're not on a 3D telly, but obviously if you are on a 3D telly, I hope you enjoy this, because they're not that common, 3D videos in thoracic surgery, but we were fortunate enough to borrow this beautiful 3D 10mm scope to do this left-fax diaphragm placation.
Now, we thought that one real advantage of 3D is that you're going to be able to see the suturing in much better detail.
This is my usual standard VAC approach, so high camera behind the scapula.
This is the anterior 10mm port, and this is the posterior 5mm port at about the 7th intercostal spaces.
I use 5mm instruments to assess the diaphragm using CO2 at about 6 to 8mm of mercury.
And here you can see
A very floppy diaphragm.
It's got loads of redundant tissue.
You can see the tendinous portion and the muscular portion.
The very most important thing of a diaphragm plication is to do the assessment of how tight you're going to make it.
So really do spend some time on this.
And the first suture is absolutely the most important one.
So see how tight you can get it.
You want to see the muscular fibers really tight above and below where you're going to do your first plication stitch.
We love the Cavidian Endo Stitch.
This uses a 2-0 non-absorbable braided suture and we put a bespoke Teflon pledget by the side.
So we do horizontal mattress sutures and as you can see the Endo Stitch does a beautiful job of stitching by vats.
We put our pledges on either side as we think that the diaphragm is very delicate and it is under a lot of strain when the patient wakes up.
So we don't want these to tear.
We've not had a single one tear.
This first particular one we did, we didn't manage to do a horizontal mattress because the needle bent a little bit.
It's quite delicate because it would be an endo stitch, so you've got to treat it very gently.
So we just did a simple first suture for this one, but then did all the others as horizontal mattress.
Until you've done your first stitch or first couple of stitches, the diaphragm is still quite high, so the lung does tend to get in the way.
But the more you do, the less the lung gets in the way.
So now we're going to do our second suture using, again, a horizontal mattress.
You'll notice I'm using a Maryland Grasper.
It's really useful because you can actually suture around it.
It's vastly better than using any heavier graspers because you can't lift up and then suture around it.
So this is something that I have found very useful.
Just one of those small tips.
I've done a long line of intercostal blocks as well along the intercostal nerves at the start of the procedure so that the patient doesn't get much pain.
So we've basically got a 10mm port just behind the scapula as high as we can.
We've put CO2 in the chest and a 5mm port to the left and a 10mm port to the right.
The cavitium end of the stitch needs a 10mm port.
So here's our third suture.
What you can also really appreciate is that big space under the diaphragm that you're making.
This means that it's absolutely impossible for you to suture any abdominal organs like the spleen or some large bowel because you can see that it's doubled over already.
There's absolutely no chance that there's any abdominal tissue in that little thin fold that you can see there.
We've not had a single case of abdominal suturing and we've done over 20 of these now.
We like to use a knot pusher.
You can actually tie knots using the endo stitch.
We just find it a bit quicker just to use a knot pusher.
And here's our fourth.
I find an intramedial to postural lateral long line of horizontal mattress sutures is my preferred way of doing these.
There are lots of other ways of doing sutures in the literature, but I find this the best to get a really nice long tight line and to distribute the load.
Sometimes you get a little bit of bleeding, but suturing it, pushing it down and tying it usually stops it.
The diaphragm is quite a vascular muscle.
even when it's paralysed.
We usually do about six to eight knots, but I'm only going to show a couple here in the video to prevent you from dire boredom.
Here again you can see the lovely overlapping of the two sides as we do our plication lines.
I think it is important to do the first couple right in the middle and then whether you do the ones laterally or medially after that doesn't really matter.
I think a long straight line, always trying to tighten the diaphragm is really important though.
You want to distribute the load between all these horizontal mattress sutures.
You don't want to have an uneven distribution because then you're going to increase the chance that some will cut through.
That's our fifth suture.
We tend to do about eight to ten horizontal mattress sutures.
And you can see once you've set it up nicely though, the place where you're going to do our next suture is very obvious.
It is tempting to not
continue to keep it tight the whole time and you can accidentally leave it a little bit loose.
So do always try and make the next stitch just a bit tighter than the last by going a bit further more laterally than you were planning.
These operations are taking about 45 minutes to an hour these days, which is very good, a vast improvement on doing diaphragm placations by thoracotomy.
And obviously as we're not removing a sample like we do in the bectomy, there are no large holes or utility incisions.
I've placed two drains that you'll see at the end.
One large one for air that comes out, that's a 20 bench drain, that comes out the next day, but I do like to leave
an 18-punch drain for three to four days.
There is an incidence of pleural effusion with these patients because they've got a paralyzed diaphragm and lots of Teflon.
And so I think this does cause a bit of reaction.
If you don't leave a drain in for a few days, you'll get people coming back with pleural effusions.
It's not a problem, it's just they don't
They don't recover as rapidly as you'll otherwise see.

Most of my patients do say the minute they wake up, they notice they can take a bigger breath, which is really very satisfying and very pleasing for them to know that they've had a successful operation straight away.
Some patients pre-operatively can't even lie flat at night, and they get their first night laying flat immediately post-operatively.
He's now doing some of the medial placation stitches as we come to the end.
We think that diaphragm placation, they are a forgotten group of patients.
We think that it's such an easy operation now and people do so well and this is the final result that really we do need to be getting out there and finding these patients.
There's a huge amount that previously we've told they're not suitable.
Now you can see really an extremely tight placation line.
There really is no more ability for us to tighten that.
That is a perfect result and we're very pleased with this and the patient did very well afterwards.
And here's the 20 French drain going anteriorly up to the apex and the 18 French posteriorly.
Thank you very much for watching.
I hope the 3D was nice.
I certainly enjoyed using it.
And go and look on my YouTube channel for other diaphragm placation videos including bats and robotics.
Thank you very much.
